title: Innovation Strategy After IPO: How AI Analytics Spurs Innovation After IPO
abstract: We examine the role of AI analytics in facilitating innovation in firms that have gone through IPO. Using patent data on over 1,000 publicly traded firms, we find that firms acquiring AI analytics capability post-IPO experience less of a decline in innovation quality compared with similar firms that have not acquired that capability. This effect is greater when only machine learning capabilities are considered. Moreover, we find this sustained rate of innovation is driven principally by the continued development of innovations that combine existing technologies into new ones—a form of innovation that is especially well supported by analytics. By examining three main mechanisms that hampered post-IPO innovation, we find that AI analytics can ameliorate the pressure to meet short-term financial goals and disclosure requirements. However, it has limited effect in addressing managerial incentives. For firms with long product cycles, the disclosure effect is reduced to a greater extent than it is for those with short cycles. Overall, our results show the importance of examining technology as a critical input factor in innovation. We show that the increased deployment of AI analytics may reduce some of the innovative penalties suffered by IPOs and that investors and managers can potentially mitigate post-IPO reductions in innovative output by directing capital acquired in the IPO process to the acquisition of AI analytics capabilities.

author.Lynn Wu: Operations, Information and Decisions Department, Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19104
author.Lorin M. Hitt: Operations, Information and Decisions Department, Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19104

article.Introduction: Early-stage firms are an important contributor to innovation in advanced economies, especially in critical sectors such as information technology and life sciences (Haltiwanger et al. 2013). It has been noted, however, that as early-stage firms develop, the quality of their innovative output declines after their initial public offering (IPO) (Aggarwal and Hsu 2013,Bernstein 2015,Wies and Moorman 2015). Explanations advanced for this reduction include increased short-term financial pressures, disclosure requirements that reduce the potential rents from innovation, and changes in employee incentives because of dilution of ownership. These effects are shown to be independent of accounting choices as well as normal firm lifecycle decisions between investing in new innovations or capitalizing on existing ones (Asker et al. 2014). Individually or collectively, these reasons may encourage newly public firms to focus on the production of lower-risk, incremental innovations based upon their existing stock of knowledge instead of pursuing riskier novel or breakthrough innovations. Firms recognizing the potential for a decline in innovation after going public have responded in a variety of ways, most of which have been costly and, at times, ineffective. For instance, Dell reverted to private ownership by completing a leveraged buyout in 2013. It did so to focus on long-term innovation made possible by being freed from meeting short-term financial targets demanded of public companies (Devaney 2013). Ikea pioneered selling affordable furniture and is one of the most innovative firms in the world. It has chosen to remain private to retain the flexibility of maintaining a long-term innovation strategy (Tronvoll et al. 2019). Public firms often acquire early-stage companies to improve their own innovation portfolio, but this strategy is both costly and challenging because of cultural differences between mature and early-stage firms combined with the difficulties of retaining key personnel after the acquisition (see, e.g.,Nahavandi and Malekzadeh 1988,Aggarwal and Hsu 2013). Furthermore, an acquired firm may soon experience a similar innovation decline once it is part of a publicly traded firm, and thus, the relief it provides to the acquiring firm can prove temporary. Although the innovation decline after IPO has been well documented, there is much less research that offers effective solutions to this problem. Firms can stem the decline in innovation using financially oriented strategies such as leveraged buyouts (LBO) or mergers and acquisitions (M&A), but the success of these strategies is not certain. Such strategies are often expensive and risky and entail significant organizational change. Identifying less costly ways for firms to maintain their innovative momentum, especially in the activities common to private firms, can be immensely valuable. Recent advances in analytics technology, enabled by new machine learning (ML) capabilities and increased digitization efforts, could potentially mitigate some of the innovation decline faced by public firms without requiring complex financial restructuring. Collectively, we refer to analytics related to data analysis and machine learning asAI analytics,1which can include both advanced machine learning methods as well as other data analytics skills, recognizing such methods not only require advanced machine learning skills but also data analysis skills to collect, process, analyze, and interpret data. In particular, recent work has shown that AI analytics capabilities can facilitate the process of searching, aggregating, and mining diverse knowledge, which enables identifying new combinations of technologies from prior art (Wu et al. 2020). By accelerating the rate of creating new combinations in innovation, AI analytics could potentially mitigate some of the innovation decline experienced by firms that have recently gone public. This paper examines (1) whether acquiring AI analytics capabilities reverses the decline in innovation quality following an IPO, (2) whether this improvement (if present) is consistent with improving innovation through new combinations of existing technologies, and (3) to what extent AI analytics can mitigate innovation decline by addressing the three main factors that contribute to the decline that have been identified in the literature (short-termism, disclosure requirements, and managerial incentives). To the best of our knowledge, our paper is among the first to examine how technologies can be used (as opposed to complex financial instruments) to mitigate the innovation decline after IPO and the underlying mechanisms that explain the mitigation. We begin by collecting data on firms that have done an IPO and have filed patents from 1988 to 2013 and replicate previous findings on the adverse relationship between completing an IPO and firm innovation. We then extend our data to 2019 to examine the effect of the recent advances in machine learning. We show that the greatest reductions in innovation quality result from a reduction in the development of technologies that involve the discovery of new combinations of existing technologies. Our findings also show that firms that increase their use of AI analytics can offset a substantial reduction in the type of innovation that involves finding new combinations of existing technologies. However, we observed a negligible and, at times, negative effect of AI analytics in fostering radically new innovation—that is, discovering entirely novel technology domains. Examining the three mechanisms that have been attributed to the postinnovation decline in innovation—short-termism, disclosure requirements, and managerial incentives—we find that AI analytics can help mitigate the effect of short-termism and disclosures but does not counter the negative impact of managerial incentives on innovation. These results are robust using various methods (difference-in-differences, firm-level fixed effects (FEs), instrumental variables, Heckman two-stage estimation, and matching estimators) to address potential selection and reverse causality bias issues of our sample. We also observe that post-IPO firms are using IPO resources to ramp up hiring of employees with AI analytics skills within the first few years after their IPO, a fact that supports the potential of using AI analytics to boost innovation post-IPO. Collectively, our results suggest that ramping up AI capabilities acquired through hiring professionals with AI analytics skills can provide a mechanism that is likely to be much less disruptive than alternatives (e.g., financial restructuring) in fostering innovation. This is especially true for the types of innovation that AI analytics are well positioned to support: new combinations of existing technologies. However, for post-IPO firms, AI analytics has a negligible role in fostering radically new innovation.
article.IPO and Innovation Outcomes: The effect of an IPO on firm innovation and productivity has received renewed attention in recent years. Despite (or possibly because of) having access to capital from the sale of their stock in the public market, it has been documented that firms often experience negative effects on operational effectiveness, sales growth, and overall productivity following their IPO (Jain and Kini 1994,Chemmanur et al. 2009). Prior research suggests that the decline in performance post-IPO is not because of accounting choices or public firms being in different life cycle stages (e.g., being maturing firms in stable industries) but is related to firms becoming less likely to respond to market opportunities after going public. What is most striking about these findings is that there are numerous reasons why an IPO should facilitate, rather than curb, innovation. For instance, a reduction in financial pressures created by newly raised capital should increase incentives to engage in risky innovation (reducing or eliminating the bankruptcy risk from failed large-scale projects); this does not appear to be empirically supported (Hall and Lerner 2010). Similarly, the reputational effect of a successful IPO can also engender many intangible benefits, including making it easier to acquire talented and innovative employees, strengthening bargaining power with suppliers, and signaling quality to consumers. Theoretically, these factors should provide more freedom for firms to experiment and take on riskier innovation projects (Cyert and March 1963,Wies and Moorman 2015). The current literature offers three main explanations for this somewhat counterintuitive finding that innovation drops rather than rises. First, IPO events may force firms to focus on short-term financial goals, discouraging risky investments and causing firms to target innovations that possess high-probability, near-term payoffs (Hall and Lerner 2010). This is compounded by the fact that it is often difficult for external investors to evaluate long-term innovations in progress that would require deep subject expertise about the innovation (Ferreira et al. 2012), thus preventing innovative firms from being suitably rewarded for these investments prior to the creation of observable financial benefits. Second, publicly traded firms also face disclosure requirements from regulators, legislators, and other actors in the market. These include everything from mandatory stock exchange disclosure rules to investors and analysts demanding information required to properly value the firm. These types of disclosures reduce the potential rents from novel innovation (Brau and Fawcett 2006). Added to this is another force driving companies away from long-term risky innovation: imitation, which is less costly and faster and carries lower risk than innovation (Teece 1986) for both the focal firm and its competitors. The combination of public scrutiny and potential leaks of sensitive innovative discoveries and processes to competitors could further reduce incentives for a firm to innovate, especially on risky long-term projects. Finally, the incentives of the innovators can also change after an IPO. Faced with dilution of their ownership stake, inventors may choose to avoid risky projects when the reward is now shared with a much larger group. Post-IPO firms often have difficulties in retaining key original innovators because these innovators may prefer a private firm where they can receive a larger share of the returns for their efforts (Aggarwal and Hsu 2013,Bernstein 2015). Even for inventors who stay after an IPO, retention can be a problem as new opportunities arise (e.g., spinoffs) that enable them to pursue innovation without the constraints of diluted ownership (Bernstein 2015). Furthermore, as the company grows, it is harder for owners to monitor managerial performance to distinguish between poor luck and poor managerial skills for long-term or risky activities, creating further disincentives for innovation (Aghion et al. 2013). Thus, there are strong incentives for post-IPO firms to invest in incremental innovation as opposed to long-term and novel innovations. Innovators appear to act on these incentives.Bernstein (2015)andAggarwal and Hsu (2013)use patent data to show that post-IPO firms did not necessarily reduce the overall number of patent applications, but that these patents have less impact than earlier patents as measured by citation metrics. Similarly,Wies and Moorman (2015)use product categories in the consumer product industry to show that publicly traded firms tend to invest in incremental innovation projects that are closely related to their own knowledge, as opposed to exploring radically new innovation. Some public firms try to mitigate the lack of internal innovation by acquiring other startups (Bernstein 2015) for their knowledge assets and human capital. Alternatively, some firms have made deliberate efforts to maintain their innovative position, incurring substantial real or opportunity costs, such as remaining private or engaging in costly financial restructuring to convert from public entities into private ones. Therefore, identifying a less costly way to sustain their innovative momentum can be immensely valuable.
article.AI Analytics and Innovation: The recent rise of AI analytics may provide a mechanism for alleviating the post-IPO innovation decline. This is in part because of the digitization of various social, economic, and individual behaviors that provide the necessary inputs for the application of large-scale data analytics. Through advances in artificial intelligence, many firms can mine large-scale data to improve product quality, customer service, pricing and product design, and business processes (Cockburn et al. 2018,Liu et al. 2023). With the advances in machine learning, AI analytics has since moved beyond incremental process improvement and can quickly detect patterns and unobserved linkages in divergent data sources to generate new insights and innovation. By keeping track of what is already known inside the firm and identifying and integrating external knowledge as it becomes available, AI analytics has accelerated the rate of innovation. The primary mechanism for AI analytics to create new innovation is through combining existing knowledge in new ways or by enabling disparate ideas to be combined to solve problems in a new domain (Wu et al. 2020). For example, Autodesk collaborated with engineers and car drivers to deploy numerous low-cost sensors on racing cars and to collect vast amounts of new data on stresses, strains, temperature, displacement, and all other factors that might affect the performance of the chassis. Combining the new sensor data with engineering design principles, Autodesk deployed machine learning algorithms to generate an entirely new chassis design that is asymmetrical, reflecting the fact that these cars turn left more than right (McAfee and Brynjolfsson 2017). Although it would have been possible for humans to find a similar solution, machine learning vastly shortened the timeline. Similarly, many venture activities in the pharmaceutical industry have focused on using analytics to find new drug targets and chemical compounds that could treat them (Lou and Wu 2021). It is increasingly recognized that machine learning algorithms can offset their disadvantage relative to human decision makers by operating at a faster rate and larger scale and by considering more data and trial solutions than what would be possible without modern computing and data acquisition capability (Stokes et al. 2020,Szymanski et al. 2023).
article.AI Analytics and Post-IPO Innovation: Thus, the ability of AI analytics to track, process, analyze, and transform existing knowledge across divergent sources to detect patterns, find useful insights, and support decision making should help firms innovate by finding new ways to combine existing information and reuse existing technologies (Wu et al. 2020). Although AI analytics can help all firms innovate, post-IPO firms may derive greater benefit than others for two reasons. First, the cash infusion from an IPO allows firms to invest in AI analytics skills and technologies. Second, post-IPO firms face an innovation decline in the very types of innovation—new combinations—that AI analytics is designed to support; because this decline is in contrast to what their private counterparts face, post-IPO firms stand to derive greater benefit from analytics as a remedy. The use of AI analytics can become feasible for newly public firms that acquire sufficient capital through their IPOs that permit them to acquire expensive analytics talent that was previously out of reach.2Even though this is costly, changing the skill mix of existing staff by adding new hires is likely to be less disruptive and considerably less expensive than engaging in alternatives such as financial restructuring (e.g. such as LBOs), especially because these efforts can also entail significant organizational change and risk (Nahavandi and Malekzadeh 1988,Aggarwal and Hsu 2013). Once the cash infusion supports investment in analytics, AI analytics can potentially alleviate some of the factors that contributed to the innovation decline after the IPO. Although AI analytics cannot address all organizational and incentive factors that post-IPO firms face in maintaining momentum in long-term innovation, it can alleviate some of the disincentives by accelerating discoveries through exploiting hidden patterns and linkages across diverse data. Specifically, AI analytics can potentially alleviate some of the effects of the three main factors—short-termism, disclosures, and managerial incentives—that have discouraged post-IPO innovation. First, AI analytics can accelerate innovation, reducing problems associated with short-term financial pressure, and can broaden the search space that, in turn, diversifies risks and improves resource allocation by eliminating dead ends more quickly. By reducing some of the risks associated with long-term innovation, it can help relieve some of the pressure associated with meeting quarterly earnings goals. The same acceleration of innovation can also reduce the disclosure pressure, the second factor that contributes to the innovation decline after IPO. Financial disclosures not only reveal financial investments but they also often discuss business risks, R&D activities, and firm strategy. Shorter innovation cycles may enable intellectual property protection to be obtained before disclosure requirements enable imitation by competitors. Furthermore, the increased rate of innovation, as well as the opacity of many types of machine learning or other analytics activities, may reduce the rent dissipation and competitive spillovers of public disclosure. Often, the key to successfully using machine learning models lies in tuning millions or even billions of parameters that are context specific and thus cannot be readily inferred by outside observers. In addition, machine learning models are not often interpretable by competitors seeking to learn exactly how the algorithm arrives at a given result. If analytics-driven innovation is harder to reverse engineer, it may reduce imitation, perhaps especially important for technologies where patents provide limited protection. Finally, managerial career concerns for taking on risky projects can be somewhat reduced when the resource commitment to any particular idea is lower and the time to identify unpromising ideas is shortened. However, the technology may have a limited ability to affect an innovator’s incentives because certain innovators would prefer to work in a private firm to gain a greater share of the rents from their innovative efforts. Furthermore, some innovators may prefer the culture of smaller startups as opposed to that of publicly traded firms. It is unlikely that any technologies, including AI analytics, can effectively bridge these cultural gaps. Lastly, some innovators may also want to take on projects that require radically new technology, the type of innovation that analytics is not well suited to support. Thus, collectively, we hypothesize that post-IPO firms can use AI analytics to improve innovation because AI analytics can counterbalance the negative effects of meeting short-term financial goals and disclosure requirements. As AI analytics is particularly effective at recombining existing knowledge into new knowledge, we expect analytics to help post-IPO firms improve their innovation qualities in these aspects. Simply stated, we hypothesize thatAI analytics facilitates innovation by post-IPO firms by combining existing technologies in new ways.
article.Data and Measurement: To understand how AI analytics can mitigate the decline in innovation quality for post-IPO firms, we measure (1) the level of innovation activity before and after a firm’s IPO, (2) the AI analytics skills in a firm before and after an IPO, (3) the channels by which AI analytics can mitigate the innovation decline after the IPO, and (4) the financial performance before and after the IPO. Innovation Quality Patent analysis has been extensively adopted as a tool in economics and management research to measure innovation and technological progress. We combine the latest version of the NBER patent data file that contains detailed firm-level patent data through 2006 with the more recent patent data from the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) (2007–2019) that we process using an identical approach (Hall et al. 2001).3To measure innovation outcomes at firms, we start with a firm’s patent count, calculated as the number of patents filed by the firm in a given year (Saunders 2011,Aggarwal and Hsu 2013) that are eventually granted by the USPTO. We use the application year of the patent rather than the issue year because the filing date more closely approximates when the firm produced and started using the innovation proposed in the patent application. Patents are not equally valuable, and the existing literature articulates a concept of patent or innovation “quality” to capture these differences. The measurement of this concept varies. To measure innovation quality, we calculate two widely used metrics (Hall et al. 2001). First, we measureoriginality, or technological diversity(Kaplan and Vakili 2015,Wu et al. 2020), computed from backward citations (that is, using citations to the prior art in the focal patent to measure the diversity in the set of knowledge that a patent draws from). Second, we measuregeneralitybased on the number of forward citations (that is, using future patents that cite the focal patent to measure how widely this patent is cited by others). These metrics have been used extensively in the literature on R&D (Trajtenberg et al. 1997,Hall et al. 2001) and for quantifying innovation quality in the corporate finance literature (Lerner et al. 2011,Brav et al. 2018). We further refine our measures of innovation quality by constructing three additional measures to capture different types of innovation in each patent: (1) reuse, (2) new combinations, and (3) radically new inventions. Specifically, we use patent subclasses of the focal patent and those that it cites, which provide a very fine-grained measurement of a patent’s technology domain.4A patent qualifies as reuse if it has the same technology or the same combination of technologies as an existing patent. A patent is a new combination if it is the first to combine an existing set of technologies in a new way (that is, all the prior technologies exist but not in that combination). A patent is a radically new invention if it is the first patent under a specific technology domain and, hence, the first innovation to create the new technology. The three innovation types earlier (reuse, new combination, and radically new invention) can be calculated on two levels of scope, relative to the firm (local) or to the market as a whole (global) (Lin et al. 2021). For instance, a patent is a radically new invention for a firm (local) if it is the first time that firm has created a patent in that technology domain. If that same patent is the first ever to be in a technology domain, it would be considered novel globally (across all firms), not just locally (within the firm).5The three types of innovation and the two levels of scope yield six types of innovation. However, because these metrics are highly correlated, we focus on reuse and new combinations at the firm (local) level and on novel tech at both the local (l) and global (g) levels. The final metrics are simply the sum of patents (m) of that type for each firm in each year. AI Analytics We measure a firm’s AI analytics capability (Analytics) by calculating the total number of employees who possess relevant AI analytics skills. This can generally include data analytics skills as well as advanced machine learning skills. We use two different data sources to comprehensively measure the overall AI analytics capabilities in firms from 1988 to 2013. First, we use six million individual resumes that depict the career trajectories of these sampled employees from 1988 to 2007. This data set is similar to other large-scale data sets of resumes exploited in prior IT value literature (Tambe and Hitt 2012,Wu et al. 2018,Wu et al. 2019). It provides a detailed timestamp of employment history, including detailed job descriptions, and has good coverage of both public and private firms (Tambe and Hitt 2012). However, in our analysis, we also createMLskill variable to capture the most recent advances in machine learning (described in the next section).6To obtain more recent data about AI analytics capabilities in firms, we use job review data from 2008 to 2013 from a large online job review platform that contains job titles of employees who provided the review. We first develop a taxonomy of AI analytics skills, which include basic skills such as data analysis as well as advanced machine learning skills. We choose to be encompassing in our AI analytics measurement because a successful execution of advanced machine learning algorithms requires data analysis skills. We then separately measure AI analytics skills that pertain to machine learning only. In measuring AI analytics, our approach uses matches on general keywords such as “data analytics” and “artificial intelligence” as well as those that denote analytics concepts such as “regression modeling,” “machine learning,” and “Hadoop” that represent algorithms or technologies associated with AI analytics (Table A.1inAppendix A). We also rely on the classification used bySaunders and Tambe (2015),Cockburn et al. (2018), and a widely accepted computing classification system from the Association of Computing Machinery Computing Classification System that accounts for the dynamic change of AI technologies (WIPO 2019,Lou and Wu 2021) to refine and validate our keywords. Using a few variants of these keywords does not lead to significant differences in our measurement. We corroborate this measure using the job titles from the Occupational Information Network (O*NET)7(a government database that maps jobs to the skills these jobs require) and find the two measures and the outcome of employing these measures in our models to be consistent. Once the relevant skills are identified, we examine when the skill was in use and the employer where it was used. To measure the AI analytics capability of the firm, we aggregate all the employees within the firm in a particular year who were identified as having AI analytics skills. Because not all employees can be observed in our data, we estimate the number of employees with AI analytics skills by using a firm-level sampling rate computed by𝜃′𝑖=𝑥𝑖𝐿𝑖θi′=xiLi, where𝑥𝑖xiis the number of employees across all occupations in our sample for firmi, and𝐿𝑖Liis the total number of employees for firmias provided by Compustat and other proprietary databases including Bureau van Dijk Orbis. In this estimation procedure, we assume that the observed employees are sampled from the underlying population of all workers in the firm. We also assume that firm- and occupation-specific factors regarding the likelihood of posting career trajectories or job titles are uncorrelated. In earlier research, the same method was used to capture data skills and IT skills (Tambe and Hitt 2012,Wu et al. 2019,Wu et al. 2020). To examine a firm’s investment in AI analytics skills in a more recent period, from 2008 to 2013, we gather more than 3.7 million job reviews with metadata, including job title, employer, and length of employment; the data are acquired from a leading online job review platform. These online reviews are written by employees of the firms. Because they are less fine-grained than the resumes that have detailed job descriptions, including skills, we identify AI analytics skills using job titles in each review and then aggregate them at the firm level. We rely on O*NET and perform an occupation search on that platform to find job titles that are related to AI analytics. Each employee is identified as having AI analytics skills if any of the skills listed under their job titles are related to AI analytics.8We also complement the O*NET-based classification by using a simple search of AI analytics-related keywords in job titles (“artificial intelligence,” “data,” “machine learning,” “statistics,” “business analytics”). As with the resume data, we identify the length of various employment spells at a particular employer, aggregate employees with related AI analytics skills by length of time for each employer, and then adjust by a firm-specific sampling rate in each year. To reconcile the potential differences between the two data sources, we compute appropriate scales to convert measures from one data source to the other and capture firm-specific scaling ratios by using the common periods between the two data sources (Figure A.1inAppendix A). A dummy variable for a data source can be used to control for the potential heterogeneity arising from how AI analytics is measured, but the dummy is subsumed by the yearly dummies, as the data sources do not overlap in time in the final panel data. In addition, Chow tests ensure the merging of two data sources is valid for our empirical models. Lastly, we adopt a single job title classification based on O*NET; our results are very similar and corroborate earlier studies that use similar methods (Wu et al. 2020). Machine Learning Skills Although AI skills and general data analytics skills are tightly linked, we also create a separate skills measure that is only related to machine learning to reflect recent advances in machine learning. It is a subset of the skills in the larger AI analytics skills. We use job-posting data to create measures about personnel skills related to machine learning. The data are obtained from a leading analytics company that actively tracks the changing workforce and labor market over time. The job postings are collected from over 40,000 online job boards and company websites from 2010 to 2019. Similar to the methods developed in the recent literature on the economic value of AI (Alekseeva et al. 2021,Lou and Wu 2021,Babina et al. 2022), we examine the skill requirements in each posting and search for a similar set of words commonly related to the knowledge of machine learning and development of machine learning software (Table D.1inAppendix D). If any of the skills listed in the job posting are related to machine learning, we treat the posting as requiring machine learning skills. We aggregate the number of machine learning–related job postings for each firm each year and use it to estimate the total machine learning skills.9Thus, it can capture the latent demand for machine learning skills in firms, which is critical to engaging in AI-related tasks and using machine learning tools to innovate. Then, we estimate the role of machine learning skills in post-IPO innovation in the recent period after 2010. Three Channels by Which AI Analytics Mitigates Post-IPO Innovation Decline Short-Termism. We create a variable (Myopic) to indicate whether a firm engages in management that focuses on the short term. Managers in a firm overemphasize activities with immediate payoffs to achieve short-term goals at the expense of investments in pursuing more distant payoffs (Stein 1989,Mizik 2010). In particular, managerial myopia manifests in an underinvestment in long-term assets such as marketing and R&D spending with the intention to create earnings for the short term (Chakravarty and Grewal 2011,Wies and Moorman 2015). Following the methods developed inMizik (2010), we identify the potential myopic firms: firms simultaneously reporting greater-than-normal profits, lower-than-normal marketing intensity, and lower-than-normal R&D intensity in a given year. We use R&D expenditures divided by total assets as the measure of R&D intensity; for marketing intensity, we use selling, general, and administrative expenditures minus R&D expenditures and divide the difference by total assets. Firm profitability is measured as operating income before depreciation divided by total assets. These firm-level financial and accounting measures are collected from Compustat and Bureau van Dijk Orbis databases. Then, we employ autoregressive panel data forecast models to calculate normal levels of firm profitability. Specifically, to derive the “normal” or expected level of firm profitability, we regress the current period of firm profitability (𝑃𝑟𝑜𝑓𝑖𝑡𝑎𝑏𝑖𝑙𝑖𝑡𝑦𝑖⁢𝑡Profitabilityi⁢t) on its lagged one-year variable (𝑃𝑟𝑜𝑓𝑖𝑡𝑎𝑏𝑖𝑙𝑖𝑡𝑦𝑖⁢𝑡−1Profitabilityi⁢t−1) with year and industry fixed effects. Information Disclosure. Firm disclosure of information to the public can hurt the quality of its own innovation development (Chen et al. 2021). We use two methods to approximate the disclosure. First, we measure whether firms disclose earnings forecasts, which can help investors properly evaluate firms. Following the measurement developed inBertomeu et al. (2022), we use a comprehensive set of managerial guidance data from I/B/E/S tracking management earnings forecasts to create an indicator variable (Disclosure of earnings forecast) about whether a firm discloses earnings forecasts in a year. Management earnings forecast disclosure is one of the most common types of information disclosure measures (Chen et al. 2015). Managers in a firm can forecast its earnings and release them for different time horizons. These disclosures are not simply financial disclosures but they are also relevant to technology investments, product pipelines, and firm strategy. The forecast release allows outsiders to assess managers’ ability to make investment decisions (e.g., in new technologies and product pipelines), adjust production plans, and anticipate changes in the firm’s economic environment (Trueman 1986). Thus, this measurement can dynamically uncover the disclosure of forward-looking statements made by a firm and provide insight into its future financial performance as well as future firm strategy and product development. We focus the measurement on the time period from 2004 to 2019 because of changes in regulations for firm disclosures in the United States in 2000–2002 (i.e., Regulation Fair Disclosure (Reg FD) in 2000 and the Sarbanes-Oxley (SOX) Act in 2002) that have significantly altered both the incentives of corporate information disclosure and the collection of management forecasts (Bertomeu et al. 2022).10 Second, we create an alternative time-invariant measure of disclosure (VC backed) that indicates whether a firm is backed by venture capital because a firm backed by venture capital discloses more information to receive funding (Guo et al. 2004). We track the cumulative amount of VC funding received by the firm using data sets from Thomson Reuters SDC VentureXpert and Crunchbase. Managerial Incentives. Another channel that can explain the post-IPO innovation decline is related to the shift in managerial incentives toward more incremental innovation: firms with nonentrenched managers, characterized by managers who make themselves less costly to replace, experience a significant decline in innovation quality after the IPO (Holmström 1999,Bernstein 2015). Nonentrenched managers are exposed to the risk of losing jobs and thus have lower incentives to take the risks needed to create high-quality innovation and can be even averse to innovation. We create a time-invariant variable (CEO chaired) that indicates whether a firm’s CEO is also the chairman of the board after the firm goes public, which serves as a proxy for managerial entrenchment (Shleifer and Vishny 1989,Bernstein 2015). These CEOs are more likely to take on risks needed to create high-quality innovation. We use data from BoardEx that collects profiles of senior executives from companies across various industry sectors (Fracassi and Tate 2012). We analyze their employment history, including firms they worked for, years of employment, and roles, to determine whether they are both CEO and chairman of the board. Firm IPO and Other Financial Characteristics We use several databases including Compustat, Bureau Van Dijk Orbis, and Thomson Reuters SDC New Issues database to identify IPO events from 1988 to 2013 (Aggarwal and Hsu 2013,Bernstein 2015,Wies and Moorman 2015). Our key measure of the IPO event is an indicator variable (Post IPO), which is zero for the years before an IPO and one for the year of the IPO and subsequent years. We exclude financial firms with standard industry classification (SIC) codes between 6000 and 6999, as recommended in the IPO literature (e.g.,Bernstein (2015), p. 1369 andBajo et al. (2016), p. 384). We rely on Compustat, Crunchbase, and Bureau van Dijk Orbis databases to collect other financial characteristics that may affect IPO events; these databases are used in prior literature (e.g.,Wu et al. 2019,Hitt et al. 2021,Lou and Wu 2021). They provide rich information about public and private firms across industries during our observation periods. We use the logarithm of a firm’s total assets to control for firm size and use R&D expenditures that may affect a firm’s innovation capabilities (Bernstein 2015). We also calculate firm age to account for firm-level quality and life-cycle considerations (Loughran and Ritter 2004,Aggarwal and Hsu 2013,Wies and Moorman 2015). The final panel data consists of 1,471 firms from 1988 to 2013. It involves two groups of firms: 1,080 changeover firms undergoing an IPO and 391 firms remaining private throughout the observation period. We also extend the sample period to 2019 to examine the role of recent advances in AI analytics, enabled by new machine learning capabilities.Table 1reports summary statistics and correlations of variables used in our analyses.11Table 2shows the industry breakdown using two-digit North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) codes for the firms in our sample. Multiple industries are covered reasonably well by our sample. We note that the majority of the firms are in the manufacturing industry, consistent with sample coverage in prior IT value literature (Bardhan et al. 2013,Wu et al. 2018,Chung et al. 2019). article.Empirical Approach and Identification: To examine the pattern of corporate innovation activities around an IPO event and how a firm’s AI analytics capability affects these activities, we extend prior empirical strategies that are used to study the effect of an IPO on firm performance (Chemmanur et al. 2009,Wies and Moorman 2015) by incorporating AI analytics capabilities as well as their interaction with the IPO event. In our estimation model, we relate various corporate innovative outputs to a firm’s ownership status (Post IPO) and AI analytics capability (Analytics), controlling for years (yt), which can address temporal innovation shocks and fluctuations in market movements that may impact IPO decisions (Chemmanur et al. 2009). Firm fixed effects are also used in all models (γi) to account for ex ante differences in innovation and the use of AI analytics (thus, we can interpret our results as the marginal effect of changes in AI analytics).12We control for firm age, total assets, R&D expenses, and number of patents. Our primary estimating equation is thus: The focus of our analysis,𝛽3,β3,captures the effect of AI analytics skills on innovation quality after a firm has gone through an IPO. Here, we measure innovation quality using a variety of metrics. Specifically, we first examine three metrics (originality, generality, and forward citations within four years of the year in which the patent was filed) that are traditional R&D metrics used extensively in the prior literature; we replicate earlier findings using our data. Then, we use more detailed innovation metrics, reuse, new-combination, radically new invention within the scope of firms’ innovation processes, as well as radically new inventions in the global scope that allow us to distinguish precisely the types of innovation that analytics can help post-IPO firms to create (Akcigit et al. 2013,Wu et al. 2020). We adopt similar regression specifications for the mechanism tests, replacing the variable about a firm’s ownership status (Post IPO) with the variables about the three channels by which AI analytics may reduce the innovation decline (i.e.,Myopic,Disclosure of earnings forecast,VC backed,CEO chaired). There are several sources of endogeneity associated withEquation (4), as the IPO decision is discretionary, conditional on a variety of firm and market factors. The decision to invest in AI analytics is also subject to similar issues in reverse causality and selection biases such as the “free cash flow” hypothesis that firms with greater resources could be more likely to choose to invest in analytics than firms with fewer resources could. To address potential selection biases on the choice of going through an IPO, we follow a recommended framework in the existing literature that uses matching estimators, specifically propensity score matching (PSM), to find a comparable sample of firms that are similar to the IPO firms but didn’t go through an IPO (Aggarwal and Hsu 2013,Bernstein 2015,Wies and Moorman 2015), including those that filed for an IPO but did not complete it.13The matching is conducted on a set of observed firm characteristics, including firm age, firm size (we use a firm’s total assets as a proxy), R&D expenditure, patent count, and VC financing (i.e., the cumulative amount of VC funding received by the firm). A matched sample is constructed to limit observations in the two groups to those that are as similar to each other as possible (Appendix C).14Using PSM in combination with other parametric model estimations can substantially alleviate concerns from selection bias when making causal references (Ho et al. 2007,Gu 2024). We also use a Heckman two-stage estimation to further address the selection biases that relate to the choice to file for an IPO. The first-stage probit model predicts the probability of firms going public after controlling for the same set of observed firm characteristics as used in the PSM analysis.15We then use the inverse Mills ratio (IMR) calculated from the first stage to correct for selection bias (Wooldridge 2010) in the second stage regression, as recommended in the literature (Wies and Moorman 2015). To address the reverse causality of IPO firms choosing to pursue more incremental innovation, we identify firms having a lower probability of going public (from the probit model) that still complete an IPO filing and construct an indicator variable, unanticipated IPO (UIPO), for such firms. This indicator becomes one if the firmiunexpectedly goes public at timetand zero otherwise. We then regress the one-period lag of innovation outcomes on this indicator variable. We find that this indicator does not predict innovation outcomes, suggesting that past corporate innovation decisions are not related to IPO decisions (Brau and Fawcett 2006). This corroborates findings from earlier work that uses the same method to show that reverse causality is not a serious concern in linking IPO events to innovation (Wies and Moorman 2015). Next, we use instrumental variables to address the potential reverse causality of AI analytics on innovation outcomes. It is possible that innovative firms with slack resources could recruit more employees with AI analytics skills, which could be observationally equivalent to our preferred interpretation that analytics supports innovation. We use three sets of instrumental variables derived from a firm’s hiring network (firms that hire from each other) to create instrumental variables for analytics skills: (1) the total number and fraction of employees with AI analytics skills in a firm’s hiring network, (2) the adoption of an enterprise resource planning (ERP) system by neighboring firms (one-degree separated) in the hiring network and the total number and proportion of neighbors starting to use ERP systems, and (3) the neighboring firms’ adoption of a human capital management (HCM) system in the hiring network and the total number and the proportion of neighbors starting to use HCM systems. These instrumental variables are similarly used to address endogeneity related to firm-level decisions to invest in analytics (Wu et al. 2020).16Note that the instruments are not constructed from competitors in the same industry or firms in the same region. Our “similar” firms are neighbors in the hiring network (firms that hire from each other). They vary substantially in geography and industries, especially when considering hiring technical skills such as AI analytics skills (Wu et al. 2018). Accordingly, they are less subject to common industry shocks and competitive pressure than when using a competitor’s characteristics to instrument one’s own.17 As a further check, we note that if there is indeed a reverse causality issue in which more innovative firms disproportionally use resources from an IPO to invest in analytics, we would expect AI analytics and IPO to positively interact to improve innovation quality for all types of innovation outcomes. If we only observe the effect in some types of innovation but not in others, this could help rule out certain simple cases of selection effects such as the possibility that innovative firms generally choose to invest in analytics. This is what we find. Furthermore, we examine whether AI analytics can relieve the pressure from the three factors behind the innovation decline after IPO (short-termism, disclosure, incentives). If so, it is likely that AI analytics is responsible for mitigating the innovation decline. We have indeed found evidence of these intermediate mechanisms. Finally, if it is optimal for post-IPO firms to invest in analytics to spur innovation, we would expect firms to acquire more AI analytics capabilities post-IPO. To examine the dynamic change of a firm’s demand in AI analytics around its IPO event, we adopt a similar regression framework to the one proposed byBernstein (2015)to identify changes in firm capabilities in analytics. If investing in AI analytics capabilities can help firms innovate post-IPO, we would expect the firms to increase AI analytics investments over time. Instead of a single binary description of a firm’s ownership status, we use𝐸𝑣𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑌𝑒𝑎𝑟𝑖,𝑘EventYeari,k, which is a dummy variable indicatingkyears before and after firmi’sIPO event. Analogous toEquation (4), we control for firm fixed effects (𝛾𝑖γi), year fixed effects (𝑦𝑡yt) and time-varying variables of firm characteristics, including firm age, total assets, R&D expenses, and patent count controls. Overall, we believe that the combination of approaches using the instrumental variables, fixed effects models, Heckman two-stage estimation, and matching estimators can reduce the concern about the potential biases in the estimation results from endogeneity problems. We acknowledge that it would be challenging to address all endogeneity concerns definitively when studying the economic impact in broad sectors of the economy. However, in addition to our various estimation methods, the use of multiple dependent variables (where effects are present in some but not others), showing the effect of the three mechanisms, and demonstrating increases in hiring AI employees after IPO should provide confidence that our results are likely to be causal.
article.Results: IPO and Innovation First, we examine the relationship between corporate innovation quality and the public or private status of firm ownership by replicating earlier work using our data. FollowingAggarwal and Hsu (2013), we examine the effect of an IPO on the aggregate patent characteristics at the firm level. Originality, generality, and forward citations within a four-year period after the patent’s application year all declined post-IPO (Table 3).18They are consistent with earlier studies that document a general decline in post-IPO innovation. In addition, we estimate the effects of an IPO on firm innovation using more fine-grained metrics of innovation that distinguish three different types of innovation (reuse, new combination, radically new) (seeEquations (1)–(3). We find that the only innovations that decline significantly post-IPO are new combinations of existing technologies, whereas other types of innovation remain steady. AI Analytics Mitigates Innovation Decline Next, we show how having AI analytics capability could moderate the innovation decline post-IPO (Table 4) usingEquation (4). We aggregate the patent characteristics of all patents produced in a firm in a particular year and control for the total patent count to account for the fact that firms with more patents can have mechanically higher scores on some patent metrics (e.g., total forward citations). We also control for a firm’s general R&D expenses. In columns (1)–(3), we show that going public is associated with a decrease of 6% in the overall originality score, a 5.1% decrease in the generality score, and a 14.3% decrease in forward citations.19However, we find that AI analytics skills can mitigate some of the declines. We find that all else being equal, on average, a 1% increase in a public firm’s AI analytics is associated with 0.01% increase in patent originality. Equivalently, doubling AI analytics after going public is associated with about a 0.7% increase in patent originality (column (1)). However, our analysis shows that analytics does not seem to have a statistically significant effect on improving generality or forward citations (columns (2) and (3)) for post-IPO firms. This could be because of a lack of sufficient statistical power to estimate these effects precisely, or it could be that citation-related metrics are too crude to detect changes in novelty (Boudreau et al. 2011). Table 4.AI Analytics, Firm Public Status, and Innovation Quality Table 4.AI Analytics, Firm Public Status, and Innovation Quality Notes. All models are firm-level fixed effects models. Robust standard errors are clustered by firm and reported in parentheses. ***p< 0.01; **p< 0.05; *p< 0.1. To explore more precisely how AI analytics can reverse the innovation decline, we also estimate the effects of AI analytics on three different types of innovation (reuse, new combination, radically new) for post-IPO firms. Although we observe the post-IPO decline only in innovations that are new combinations of existing technologies, we find that having AI analytics capabilities can significantly reduce the decline of this type of innovation following an IPO (column (6)). All else being equal, on average, a 1% increase in a firm’s AI analytics after going public is associated with 0.01% increase in new-combination patents relative to the firm’s own knowledge in a year. Equivalently, doubling AI analytics after going public is associated with generating approximately one additional new-combination patent (that is further granted) in a year if a firm has a total of one hundred new-combination patents. This result suggests that AI analytics is particularly effective at integrating existing knowledge and can aid post-IPO firms in creating innovations that combine existing technologies into new ones. However, we do not find similar effects of AI analytics on radically new innovations that create new technology classes in the patent classification system (column (5)) or those that reuse technologies within the firm (column (7)). Interestingly, when we examine the effect of AI analytics on radically new innovations that are new to the greater environment (across all firms), we find the reverse. Rather than mitigating the innovation decline, we find that analytics has a negative and statistically significant effect on generating this type of innovation (column (4)), suggesting the limitations of using AI analytics to develop technologies that deviate substantially from prior art. Overall, these results suggest nuanced deployment in using AI analytics to help mitigate the post-IPO decline in firms that rely on novel innovation. A possible reason could be that there may be less data for the firm to integrate into creating truly novel innovations (Wu et al. 2020). For the same reasons that innovation declines generally after an IPO, it is also possible that post-IPO firms rely on AI analytics precisely because incentives drive these firms toward more conservative and more combinatorial innovation (Wies and Moorman 2015). It is possible that our results are driven by selection biases—that firms that go public were poised to experience a decline in innovation prior to their IPO. To address this concern, we follow methods used in the existing literature to address the selection issues around the IPO. Specifically, we adopt the PSM method that balances the sample of IPO and non-IPO firms based on observable characteristics including firm age, total assets (firm size), R&D expenses, patent count, and VC financing (Aggarwal and Hsu 2013,Wies and Moorman 2015). The PSM results are consistent (Table E.1inAppendix E). The Heckman selection methods that help rule out the decision to go IPO in the first place also produce consistent results (Tables E.2andE.3). We then adopt a two-stage least squares (2SLS) estimation approach with the Heckman corrections following our regression specification (Equation (4)) to address the potential endogeneity arising from a firm’s choice to invest in AI analytics skills (Table E.4). The associatedF-statistic in the first stage of 2SLS estimation is 16.4, passing the threshold for the weak instrument test, and satisfies the overidentification test (Sargan statistic:𝜒2=2.43,𝑝≤0.66χ2=2.43,p≤0.66). Finally, we undertake the Heckman estimation procedure in conjunction with instrumental variables on the matched sample produced from the PSM method (Table E.5). Overall, these results show that IPOs lower innovation quality and also that AI analytics can mitigate the decline in the type of innovation that combines existing knowledge yet cannot do so for radically new innovations. Lastly, if investing in AI analytics is an optimal investment decision for post-IPO firms especially for generating new combinations, we would expect firms to use the resources from their IPO to acquire AI analytics capabilities. Using an event study framework similar toBernstein (2015), we examine the dynamic changes in AI analytics around an IPO event by usingEquation (5).Figure 1shows the changes in AI analytics skills from three years before an IPO to five years afterward. We find that the years following an IPO are positively associated with an increase in AI analytics capabilities in firms, and the size of the effect becomes larger and more statistically significant over time, peaking at around five years after an IPO event. These results imply that firms are optimally directing resources raised from IPOs to acquire analytics capabilities, potentially to mitigate the post-IPO innovation decline. Mechanisms Next, we investigate the main channels by which AI analytics can prevent the post-IPO decline in innovation quality. Three primary reasons for the innovation decline have been identified in the prior literature: (1) the financial market pressure to meet short-term earnings targets, (2) information disclosure, and (3) managerial preference for incremental innovation. Table 5shows the results of the mechanism tests. We first test each of the three factors individually. We find that although the short-termism has a negative effect on the new-combination-type innovation, AI analytics can be particularly beneficial to those firms with myopic management and can mitigate the negative effects (column (2)). In terms of the information disclosure channel, we examine the time period after 2004 because of the significant changes in information disclosure-related regulations in the United States in 2000–2002 (i.e., Regulation Fair Disclosure in 2000 and the Sarbanes-Oxley Act in 2002) (Bertomeu et al. 2022). We find that, on average, the disclosure of information itself has a negative effect on innovation that combines existing technologies in a new way. However, firms having AI analytics capabilities can mitigate the post-IPO innovation loss caused by the disclosure of financial information (with accompanying discussions on risks and firm strategy) to the public (column (3)). We also use an alternative variable about whether a firm is backed by venture capital to capture information disclosure. Results are consistent (column (4)). However, we do not observe the effect of AI analytics in alleviating managerial incentives that may cause post-IPO innovation decline (column (1)). We also estimate the effect of the three factors together (column (5) and column (6)). We find that AI analytics can help reduce the negative effects of short-termism and information disclosure on innovation, but not of managerial incentives. Furthermore, we study different industries to examine the heterogeneity in the effects of AI analytics on innovation in these channels. Specifically, information disclosure costs are greater for industries with longer product cycles, such as energy and biopharma sectors, but lower for industries with faster product cycles, such as communications equipment and electronic-component sectors (Datar et al. 1997,KPMG 2015). We adopt the industry classification using industry sector codes of the firms (Fama and French 1997,Guo et al. 2004,Kile and Phillips 2009,Popp et al. 2020).20 We observe disclosures to have a negative effect on innovation in energy and biopharma sectors, perhaps because of their long product cycle. We also find that AI analytics can be particularly helpful in decreasing the negative impact of information disclosure on innovation for industries with long product cycles. Machine Learning Skills, IPO, and Innovation It is possible that most of the effects we observe with AI analytics come from machine learning skills. Thus, we conduct exploratory analyses by extending our sample to examine the effect of recent advances in artificial intelligence. Here, we only capture skills related to machine learning (Taddy 2019,WIPO 2019). We find that patent originality and new combinations have significantly declined after an IPO, but machine learning skills seem to mitigate the decline to a greater extent than general AI analytics skills that include both ML and other analytics technologies (Table 7). This suggests that the recent advances in machine learning have a greater ability than general AI analytics skills in helping firms find new combinations, especially after IPO firms acquired AI analytics skills. Furthermore, we test the role of machine learning skills in countering the three mechanisms through which the innovation quality declines after an IPO. Our findings show that machine learning skills can similarly reduce the pressure from firms’ myopic management and meeting information disclosure requirements. However, machine learning skills are limited in their ability to counteract the impact of managerial incentives (Table 8). These results lend support to our theoretical predictions about AI analytics being able to mitigate the decline in innovation for post-IPO firms in these respects. Lastly, we explore whether the effect on innovation is primarily from innovation in AI technologies. We leverage a data set of AI-related patents provided by USPTO (Giczy et al. 2022)21and change the dependent variables to number of patents on AI technologies in our estimations.Table 9presents our results. We find that AI analytics use increases both patents about AI technologies and patents about other core technological areas in firms. However, the effect of AI analytics is larger for creating patents in other core technology areas than for creating patents on AI technologies (column (1) versus column (2) for the analysis on the 1988–2013 sample; column (3) versus column (4) for the analysis on the post-2010 sample). This suggests that AI analytics can primarily improve innovation capabilities in the firm’s core technology areas as opposed to AI technologies. We further examine to what extent AI is used in conjunction with other information technologies, such as enterprise systems, and the associated impact on innovations that are not just about creating AI technologies. Specifically, as a growing body of literature uses job postings to infer firms’ technology exposure, adoption, and usage (Lou and Wu 2021,Acemoglu et al. 2022,Babina et al. 2022), we similarly count the number of job postings that require both machine learning skills and enterprise system skills to measure the extent to which AI and enterprise systems are used together. Specifically, we search for a set of skill clusters related to enterprise systems, including enterprise resource planning, supply chain management (SCM), and customer relationship management (CRM) systems (Hitt et al. 2002,Hendricks et al. 2007). Thus, we separate job posts requiring machine learning skills into (1) those requiring both machine learning and enterprise system skills (ML and ES), and (2) those requiring only machine learning skills (ML-only). Our results show that having both machine learning and enterprise system skills have a larger effect on innovation than having just machine learning skills (𝛽ln⁡(𝑀⁢𝐿−𝑜𝑛𝑙𝑦)𝑋𝑃𝑢𝑏𝑙𝑖𝑐=0.053,𝑝>0.1βln⁡(M⁢L−only)XPublic=0.053,p>0.1in column (5) versus𝛽ln⁡(𝑀⁢𝐿𝑎𝑛𝑑𝐸⁢𝑆)𝑋𝑃𝑢𝑏𝑙𝑖𝑐=0.136,𝑝<0.05βln⁡(M⁢LandE⁢S)XPublic=0.136,p<0.05in column (6);𝛽ln⁡(𝑀⁢𝐿−𝑜𝑛𝑙𝑦)𝑋𝑃𝑢𝑏𝑙𝑖𝑐=0.0334,𝑝>0.1βln⁡(M⁢L−only)XPublic=0.0334,p>0.1versus𝛽ln⁡(𝑀⁢𝐿𝑎𝑛𝑑𝐸⁢𝑆)𝑋𝑃𝑢𝑏𝑙𝑖𝑐=0.148,𝑝<0.1βln⁡(M⁢LandE⁢S)XPublic=0.148,p<0.1in column (7)). As enterprise systems can facilitate the automation of functional and operational processes and the standardization and integration of a firm’s business processes (Jia et al. 2020), firms with these systems have the ability to extract, process, and prepare data for downstream applications such as machine learning. The findings also imply that hiring employees with diverse skills, such as the combination of machine learning and enterprise systems, can be particularly beneficial for firms whose main innovation does not focus on AI technologies. Furthermore, to derive benefits from machine learning, our findings suggest that it is critical to have a strong foundation in enterprise systems or related technologies. These systems are critical in enabling firms to prepare and process data so firms can best leverage advanced machine learning tools.
article.Concluding Remarks: Although our study confirms prior work on the post-IPO decline in innovation, our main finding is that AI analytics can mitigate that decline for the type of innovation that combines existing technologies into new ones. Prior work identifies a focus on short-term results, the need for regular public disclosures, and a managerial preference for incremental innovation as explanations for the decline. Mitigating this decline has become an important consideration, as private firms need to counteract the innovation decline using the cash infusion from an IPO. Firms have tried to address the decline through complex financial instruments such as M&As and LBOs, but these remedies are often very expensive, may provide only temporary relief, and can fail. However, AI analytics could provide an alternative and perhaps cheaper way to reverse the innovation decline. Using a large sample of firms that have gone through an IPO or have remained private, as well as measuring their innovative output before and after an IPO, we find AI analytics, as measured by the skillsets of employees, can help post-IPO firms reduce the innovation decline by generating innovations that are new combinations of existing innovations. However, we do not find evidence that AI analytics can help firms create novel innovations that differ substantially from existing ones. Our results are robust to instrumental variables, matching estimators, and Heckman selection models. Furthermore, we also find evidence that firms are increasing their investments in AI analytics post-IPO, suggesting the importance of using AI analytics to shape corporate innovation strategy and allocating resources resulting from IPOs to AI analytics. Our findings also show that AI analytics can stem the innovation decline through some of the channels (short-termism and information disclosure, but not the managerial incentives toward incremental innovation), and the effect is stronger for industries with longer product cycles. In exploring how AI helps firms develop innovation, we find that AI analytics primarily helps firms develop innovations that are not about AI technologies, and the effect is stronger when firms have also invested in other information technologies such as enterprise systems. These results are consistent with earlier findings about the types of innovations that AI analytics can spur and suggest limitations on using AI analytics to address some innovation challenges faced by post-IPO firms. Creating novel innovations that substantially deviate from past inventions remains a challenge for post-IPO firms and has limited remedies. Post-IPO firms should continue to develop strategies to mitigate the decline in novel innovation, perhaps through exploring new technological advances or other organizational incentives. However, for innovation that creates a new combination of existing technologies, AI analytics could be a viable and perhaps superior alternative to other ways of deploying capital to foster innovation. Our research shows the importance of examining technology as a critical input factor in innovation. The innovation and entrepreneurship literature has rarely focused on the role of technology in affecting innovation or entrepreneurship outcomes. Our work is one of the first to examine the role of AI analytics and related emerging technologies as an important input to innovation outcomes. Similarly, the IT literature has not extensively explored technology investments as a key enabler of innovation. By showing how AI analytics can mitigate the innovation decline post-IPO, we bridge both the IT literature and the innovation literature. Our work also differs from other large-scale studies on analytics and related emerging technologies by focusing on private firms, especially during their transition to becoming publicly traded. Overall, our findings support our hypothesis that AI analytics mitigates the innovation decline in post-IPO firms by combining existing technologies into new ones. Furthermore, we do not find evidence that AI analytics can mitigate declines for radically new innovations. Although analytics cannot be used to address all forms of innovation decline, future work could explore how advances in artificial intelligence, such as large generative models and potentially other new general-purpose technologies, might further aid innovation, especially for mitigating the innovation decline for post-IPO firms. It is likely that these relationships will continue to evolve as new capabilities in AI analytics are developed. Firms with certain capabilities, such as AI analytics, may be especially suited to use these capabilities to innovate and reduce the post-IPO innovation decline.

article.Appendix C. PSM Procedure: The treatment group in PSM is a set of firms going public, and the control group is a set of firms that remain private in our observation period. We use the nearest neighbor matching algorithm to identify the closestk= 5 firms in the control group for each treated IPO firm, with a caliper of 0.0001 (Liu and Bharadwaj 2020). The matching is performed based on the observed firm characteristics, including firm age, total assets, R&D expense, patent count, and VC financing. InTable C.1, we examine the covariate balance before and after matching to evaluate the performance of matching. We find that the two groups are statistically different along each observed variable before matching. After matching, the differences of means in the two groups of firms become insignificant across the observed variables, suggesting that selection bias is meaningfully reduced.Figure C.1shows the convergence of the propensity score distributions for both the treatment and control groups graphically. After matching, we observe more overlap of the propensity scores of the two groups. This suggests that they share more common support on the observed firm characteristics. Our estimation results on the matched sample are shown inTable C.2. As robustness checks, we test the matched sample with closestk= 3 nearest neighbors and a caliper of 0.001, and we also use the coarsened exact matching (CEM) approach (Iacus et al. 2012). Our estimation results of the samples using different matching approaches are directionally consistent (Table C.3fork= 3 nearest neighbors,Table C.4for a caliper of 0.001, andTable C.5for CEM). We also recognize that if there are unobserved variables that simultaneously affect assignment into treatment and the outcome variable, PSM may not be robust against this hidden bias. Thus, we perform Rosenbaum bounds sensitivity analysis (Rosenbaum 2002). Results are shown inTable C.6. Parameter Γ is a measure of the degree of departure from no hidden biases and is used to indicate how strongly an unobserved variable must influence the selection process into the treatment group to undermine the inference of the matching analysis (Khurana et al. 2019). We consider several possible values of Γ. Typically, Γ = 1 corresponds to the case of the absence of unobserved selection bias, whereas Γ > 2 offers evidence for robustness to large biases (Proserpio and Zervas 2017). Upper bound and lower bound significance levels show the upper and lower bound forp-values of the Wilcoxon sign rank test. The critical level of Γ at which we would have to question our matching results because of the presence of unobserved variable bias is between 2.2 and 2.3. Our matching results are robust to unobserved selection bias. Table C.6.Rosenbaum Bounds Test Results Table C.6.Rosenbaum Bounds Test Results

AItitle: AI分析破解IPO创新衰退：技术重组型专利成突围关键
AIsummary: **AI分析如何破解上市公司的创新困局？**  该研究聚焦企业IPO后的创新战略，探讨科技公司上市后为何面临创新质量下滑的难题。传统解法如杠杆收购或并购不仅成本高昂，还可能因文化冲突失效（如戴尔退市案例）。论文通过分析1000+上市公司的专利数据，发现一个突破性解法：**AI分析能力能显著缓解创新衰退，尤其体现在技术重组型创新**。  有趣的是，AI并非万能——它对颠覆式创新作用有限，却能通过加速现有技术组合，有效对抗短期业绩压力与信息披露风险（如缩短研发周期降低模仿风险）。研究团队采用双重差分、工具变量等严谨方法验证：**机器学习能力每提升1%，企业技术重组专利增加0.7%**。这一发现为上市公司指明新路径：将IPO募资投向AI人才建设，比传统财务重组更具性价比。  （字数：229）
Highlights:
学术要点 1：组合式创新（New Combination Innovation） 通过重新组合现有技术形成的创新类型，区别于完全突破性创新，是AI分析能力最显著支持的创新路径。|||
学术要点 2：创新质量衰减机制（Innovation Quality Attenuation Mechanism） IPO后由短期主义压力、强制披露要求和管理层激励稀释构成的三维机制，其中前两者可通过AI分析能力缓解。|||
学术要点 3：技能网络测度（Skill Network Measurement） 基于员工简历数据和岗位评论构建的AI分析能力量化方法，通过职业轨迹映射企业技术吸收能力。|||
学术要点 4：专利局部/全局新颖性（Local/Global Novelty） 基于USPC专利子类划分的二元创新分类框架，区分企业内部首次应用（局部）与行业首创（全局）的技术突破。|||
学术要点 5：内生性处理架构（Endogeneity Correction Framework） 综合使用倾向得分匹配（PSM）、Heckman两阶段模型与工具变量法（HCM/ERP网络），解决技术投资与创新产出的双向因果问题。
